```prisma
  tags  Tag[]   // Prisma creates junction table automatically
}

model Tag {
  id    String @id @default(uuid())
  name  String @unique
  posts Post[]
}
// Creates _PostToTag junction table automatically
```

### Many-to-Many (Explicit Junction Table)

```prisma
model Student {
  id          String       @id @default(uuid())
  name        String
  enrollments Enrollment[]
}

model Course {
  id          String       @id @default(uuid())
  title       String
  enrollments Enrollment[]
}

model Enrollment {
  id         String   @id @default(uuid())
  studentId  String   @map("student_id")
  courseId   String   @map("course_id")
  grade      String?
  enrolledAt DateTime @default(now()) @map("enrolled_at")

  student    Student  @relation(fields: [studentId], references: [id])
  course     Course   @relation(fields: [courseId], references: [id])

  @@unique([studentId, courseId])
  @@map("enrollments")
}
```

### Self-Relation

```prisma
model Employee {
  id        String     @id @default(uuid())
  name      String
  managerId String?    @map("manager_id")
  manager   Employee?  @relation("Management", fields: [managerId], references: [id])
  reports   Employee[] @relation("Management")

  @@map("employees")
}
```

## Generating and Using the Client

```bash
# Generate client after schema changes
npx prisma generate

# Push schema to DB (development only, no migration files)
npx prisma db push
```

```typescript
// Usage
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

const prisma = new PrismaClient();

// Full type safety!
const user = await prisma.user.create({
  data: {
    email: 'alice@example.com',
    name: 'Alice',
    passwordHash: 'hashed...',
  },
});
// user is fully typed: { id: string, email: string, name: string, ... }
```

## Full E-Commerce Schema Example

```prisma
model User {
  id           String    @id @default(uuid())
  email        String    @unique
  name         String
  passwordHash String    @map("password_hash")
  role         Role      @default(CUSTOMER)
  orders       Order[]
  reviews      Review[]
  addresses    Address[]
  createdAt    DateTime  @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt    DateTime  @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  @@map("users")
}

model Product {
  id          String      @id @default(uuid())
  name        String
  slug        String      @unique
  description String?
  price       Decimal     @db.Decimal(10, 2)
  stock       Int         @default(0)
  categoryId  String?     @map("category_id")
  category    Category?   @relation(fields: [categoryId], references: [id])
  images      ProductImage[]
  orderItems  OrderItem[]
  reviews     Review[]
  tags        Tag[]
  isActive    Boolean     @default(true) @map("is_active")
  metadata    Json        @default("{}")
  createdAt   DateTime    @default(now()) @map("created_at")

  @@map("products")
  @@index([categoryId])
  @@index([isActive, price])
}

model Category {
  id       String     @id @default(uuid())
  name     String
  slug     String     @unique
  parentId String?    @map("parent_id")
  parent   Category?  @relation("CategoryTree", fields: [parentId], references: [id])
  children Category[] @relation("CategoryTree")
  products Product[]

  @@map("categories")
}

model Order {
  id        String      @id @default(uuid())
  userId    String      @map("user_id")
  user      User        @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  status    OrderStatus @default(PENDING)
  items     OrderItem[]
  total     Decimal     @db.Decimal(10, 2)
  createdAt DateTime    @default(now()) @map("created_at")

  @@map("orders")
  @@index([userId])
}

enum Role {
  CUSTOMER
  ADMIN
  VENDOR
}

enum OrderStatus {
  PENDING
  PROCESSING
  SHIPPED
  DELIVERED
  CANCELLED
}
```
2.5.1.intuition: The Prisma schema is like an architect's blueprint. You draw the blueprint once (the schema), and from it you can generate the actual building (database tables via migrations) and a perfect 3D model for virtual tours (the type-safe client). Change the blueprint, regenerate, and everything stays in sync.

exercises.title: Design a Blog Schema
exercises.description: Create a Prisma schema for a blog with: users (with roles), posts (draft/published status, slug, tags), comments (nested/threaded), likes (on posts and comments). Use appropriate data types, relations, and indexes.
exercises.hint: Use enum for post status. Self-relation on Comment for threading. Explicit junction table for post-tag M:N with a created_at field.

exercises.title: Prisma Schema from Existing DB
exercises.description: Use `npx prisma db pull` to introspect an existing PostgreSQL database and generate a schema. Then modify the generated schema to add proper relations, enums, and @map annotations.
exercises.hint: Set DATABASE_URL to point to your existing database. After pulling, Prisma generates models matching your tables. Add relation fields manually.

exercises.title: Multi-Model Schema with Constraints
exercises.description: Design a schema for a project management tool: organizations, teams, users (with team membership), projects, tasks, and comments. Include proper cascading deletes and unique constraints.
exercises.hint: Use onDelete: Cascade for child records. Create explicit junction table for user-team membership with a role field.

problems.problem: Error: Prisma schema validation error - relation field must have @relation attribute
problems.cause: When you have multiple relations between the same two models, Prisma can't disambiguate them automatically.
problems.solution: Add named relations: `@relation("AuthoredPosts")` and `@relation("ReviewedPosts")` to disambiguate. Both sides must use the same name.

problems.problem: @updatedAt field doesn't update on nested writes
problems.cause: Prisma's @updatedAt only updates when the model itself is directly updated, not when related models change.
problems.solution: Manually set updatedAt in your update queries for parent models, or use a database trigger to update the timestamp.

problems.problem: Decimal values come back as Prisma.Decimal objects, not numbers
problems.cause: Prisma uses a Decimal.js wrapper to preserve precision for decimal/numeric columns.
problems.solution: Convert to number with `.toNumber()` or to string with `.toString()`. For JSON serialization, add a custom toJSON method or use a transform.

2.5.2.intuition: Prisma Client is like a personal assistant who knows every table and column in your database by heart. When you say 'find users where orders are more than 100,' the assistant not only writes the perfect query but also checks your request for mistakes at compile time — catching typos in field names, wrong filter types, and missing required fields before you even run the code.

exercises.title: Dynamic Filter Builder
exercises.description: Build a function `searchProducts(filters)` that accepts optional: search (text match on name/description), minPrice, maxPrice, categoryId, inStock (boolean), sortBy, sortDir, page, pageSize. Construct the Prisma query dynamically.
exercises.hint: Build the `where` object conditionally: `const where: Prisma.ProductWhereInput = {}; if (filters.search) where.OR = [{name: {contains: ...}}, {description: {contains: ...}}];`

exercises.title: Cursor-Based Pagination API
exercises.description: Implement a paginated API that returns items with a `nextCursor` field. The client passes the cursor to get the next page. Handle the first page (no cursor) and last page (no more items) edge cases.
exercises.hint: Use `take: limit + 1` to check if there's a next page. If you get limit+1 results, there's more data. Return the last item's id as nextCursor.

exercises.title: Aggregation Dashboard
exercises.description: Build a dashboard endpoint that returns: total users, new users this month, total revenue, revenue by status, top 5 products by order count, average order value by month for the last 12 months.
exercises.hint: Use prisma.order.aggregate for totals, prisma.order.groupBy for breakdowns. For complex queries, use prisma.$queryRaw.

problems.problem: N+1 query problem with nested includes
problems.cause: Prisma uses separate queries for each relation include (not JOINs). Including deeply nested relations generates many queries.
problems.solution: Use `select` to limit fetched data. For complex queries with multiple nested relations, consider using `prisma.$queryRaw` with manual JOINs.

problems.problem: Type error: Argument type is not assignable
problems.cause: Passing the wrong type to a Prisma query. Common with Date objects, Decimal values, or enum strings.
problems.solution: Check Prisma's generated types. Use `new Date()` for DateTime fields, `new Prisma.Decimal('10.50')` for Decimal fields, and enum values from the generated enum.

problems.problem: findUnique returns null instead of throwing
problems.cause: findUnique returns null when no record matches, which can lead to null reference errors downstream.
problems.solution: Use `findUniqueOrThrow` when you expect a record to exist. Or check for null and throw a custom 404 error.

2.5.3.intuition: Nested writes in Prisma are like filling out a form that automatically creates entries in related filing cabinets. When you create a user with orders and order items, it is like submitting a single form that the system breaks down and files in three different cabinets, all atomically. If any filing fails, all forms are returned and nothing is stored.

exercises.title: Complete Order Flow
exercises.description: Implement createOrder(userId, items[]) that: validates all products exist and have stock, creates an order with order items (using nested write), decrements stock for each product, and returns the full order with items and product details. Use Prisma transactions.
exercises.hint: Use prisma.$transaction for the multi-step logic. Nested writes handle the order + items creation. Separate update calls for stock decrement.

exercises.title: Tag Management System
exercises.description: Build functions for: addTags(postId, tagNames[]) that creates tags if they don't exist and connects them, removeTags(postId, tagNames[]), replaceTags(postId, tagNames[]), and getPostsByTag(tagName). Use connectOrCreate and set.
exercises.hint: Use connectOrCreate for adding tags. Use set: [] for replacing all tags. Use disconnect for removing specific tags.

exercises.title: Nested Query Explorer
exercises.description: Write 10 different queries that use relation filters (some, every, none), nested includes, relation counts (_count), and ordering by relation count. Document what SQL each generates.
exercises.hint: Enable query logging: `new PrismaClient({ log: ['query'] })`. Each Prisma operation generates one or more SQL queries.

problems.problem: Nested create fails with foreign key constraint error
problems.cause: Creating a related record that references a non-existent parent. Or the relation fields in the schema don't match the actual database foreign keys.
problems.solution: Use `connect` to link existing records, `connectOrCreate` to link-or-create. Verify schema matches DB with `npx prisma db pull`.

problems.problem: Nested writes generate too many queries
problems.cause: Prisma uses separate queries for relations, not JOINs. A deeply nested include with many relations can generate dozens of queries.
problems.solution: Use `select` to limit fetched fields. For performance-critical paths, use `prisma.$queryRaw` with manual JOINs. Consider denormalization for read-heavy paths.

problems.problem: disconnect doesn't work on required relations
problems.cause: You can't set a required (non-nullable) foreign key to null, which is what disconnect does.
problems.solution: For required relations, use `delete` instead of `disconnect`. Or make the relation optional in the schema if disconnection should be allowed.

2.5.4.body:
# Prisma Migrate: Creating and Running Migrations

## Introduction

Prisma Migrate creates SQL migration files from changes in your Prisma schema. Unlike Knex migrations where you write SQL/JS manually, Prisma compares your schema to the current database state and generates the migration SQL automatically. This schema-first workflow means your Prisma schema is always the source of truth.

## Migration Workflow

```
1. Edit schema.prisma
2. Run: npx prisma migrate dev --name description
3. Prisma diffs schema vs database
4. Generates SQL migration file
5. Applies migration to database
6. Regenerates Prisma Client

  schema.prisma ---> diff ---> migration.sql ---> database
       |                                            |
       +--- prisma generate ---> @prisma/client ----+
```

## Creating Your First Migration

```bash
# Generate and apply migration
npx prisma migrate dev --name init
```

This creates:
```
prisma/
  migrations/
    20240115120000_init/
      migration.sql
    migration_lock.toml
```

```sql
-- prisma/migrations/20240115120000_init/migration.sql
-- CreateEnum
CREATE TYPE "Role" AS ENUM ('CUSTOMER', 'ADMIN', 'VENDOR');

-- CreateTable
CREATE TABLE "users" (
    "id" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "email" VARCHAR(320) NOT NULL,
    "name" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "password_hash" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "role" "Role" NOT NULL DEFAULT 'CUSTOMER',
    "created_at" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    "updated_at" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL,

    CONSTRAINT "users_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
);

-- CreateIndex
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX "users_email_key" ON "users"("email");
```

## Adding Schema Changes

```prisma
// Add a new field to User
model User {
  // ... existing fields
  phone    String?   // NEW
  avatar   String?   // NEW
}
```

```bash
npx prisma migrate dev --name add_phone_and_avatar
```

Generated SQL:
```sql
-- AlterTable
ALTER TABLE "users" ADD COLUMN "phone" TEXT;
ALTER TABLE "users" ADD COLUMN "avatar" TEXT;
```

## Common Migration Scenarios

### Adding a New Table with Relations

```prisma
model Product {
  id         String   @id @default(uuid())
  name       String
  price      Decimal  @db.Decimal(10, 2)
  categoryId String?  @map("category_id")
  category   Category? @relation(fields: [categoryId], references: [id])
  @@map("products")
  @@index([categoryId])
}

model Category {
  id       String    @id @default(uuid())
  name     String    @unique
  products Product[]
  @@map("categories")
}
```

### Renaming a Column

```prisma
model User {
  // Change 'name' to 'fullName'
  fullName String @map("full_name") // rename the column
}
```

```bash
npx prisma migrate dev --name rename_name_to_full_name
# Prisma might generate DROP + ADD instead of RENAME
# Check the SQL and edit if needed!
```

**Important**: Prisma may generate a DROP COLUMN + ADD COLUMN instead of ALTER COLUMN RENAME. Always review the generated SQL for destructive changes!

### Adding NOT NULL to Existing Column

```javascript
// Step 1: Add the column as nullable with a default
// (edit the generated migration SQL if needed)

// Step 2: Backfill data
// Create a custom migration:
npx prisma migrate dev --name backfill_timezone --create-only
```

```sql
-- Edit the generated migration to include backfill:
UPDATE "users" SET "timezone" = 'UTC' WHERE "timezone" IS NULL;
ALTER TABLE "users" ALTER COLUMN "timezone" SET NOT NULL;
```

## Migration Commands

```bash
# Development: generate + apply + regenerate client
npx prisma migrate dev --name description

# Generate migration WITHOUT applying (for review)
npx prisma migrate dev --name description --create-only

# Production: apply pending migrations
npx prisma migrate deploy

# Reset database (drops all data!)
npx prisma migrate reset

# Check migration status
npx prisma migrate status

# Resolve failed migration
npx prisma migrate resolve --applied "20240115120000_init"
```

## Production Deployment

```json
// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "build": "tsc",
    "start": "node dist/index.js",
    "migrate:deploy": "prisma migrate deploy",
    "postinstall": "prisma generate"
  }
}
```

```yaml
# CI/CD pipeline
steps:
  - name: Install dependencies
    run: npm ci
  - name: Run migrations
    run: npx prisma migrate deploy
    env:
      DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
  - name: Start application
    run: npm start
```

```
migrate dev vs migrate deploy:

  migrate dev (development):
    - Creates migration files
    - Applies them
    - Regenerates client
    - Can reset database
    - Interactive prompts

  migrate deploy (production):
    - ONLY applies existing migration files
    - No file generation
    - No interactive prompts
    - Never resets database
    - Safe for CI/CD
```

## Seeding with Prisma

```typescript
// prisma/seed.ts
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
const prisma = new PrismaClient();

async function main() {
  // Clear existing data
  await prisma.orderItem.deleteMany();
  await prisma.order.deleteMany();
  await prisma.user.deleteMany();

  // Seed users
  const alice = await prisma.user.create({
    data: {
      email: 'alice@example.com',
      name: 'Alice',
      passwordHash: '$2b$10$...',
      role: 'ADMIN',
    },
  });

  console.log('Seeded:', { alice });
}

main()
  .catch(console.error)
  .finally(() => prisma.$disconnect());
```

```json
// package.json
{
  "prisma": {
    "seed": "tsx prisma/seed.ts"
  }
}
```

```bash
npx prisma db seed
# Or automatically with: npx prisma migrate reset
```
2.5.4.intuition: Prisma Migrate is like a GPS navigation system for database changes. You set your destination (the schema you want), and it calculates the route (migration SQL) from where you are (current database state) to where you want to be. In development, it drives for you. In production, it follows the pre-planned route exactly.

exercises.title: Migration Evolution
exercises.description: Starting from an empty schema, create 5 sequential migrations that: (1) create users table, (2) create posts table with user FK, (3) add tags and post_tags tables, (4) add a 'views' counter to posts, (5) rename 'views' to 'view_count'. Run migrate:latest, then migrate:rollback, then migrate:latest again.
exercises.hint: Use --create-only for step 5 to review the SQL. Prisma might try to drop and recreate instead of rename — edit the SQL manually.

exercises.title: Safe NOT NULL Migration
exercises.description: You have a users table with 100k rows and need to add a non-nullable 'timezone' column. Write a migration that: adds the column as nullable, backfills with 'UTC', then makes it NOT NULL. Test with real data.
exercises.hint: Use --create-only, then edit the SQL to include the UPDATE statement before the NOT NULL alteration.

exercises.title: CI/CD Migration Pipeline
exercises.description: Write a script that: checks migration status, applies pending migrations, runs the seed if the database is empty, and starts the application. Handle errors gracefully (failed migrations should not start the app).
exercises.hint: Use `npx prisma migrate status` to check, `npx prisma migrate deploy` to apply. Check exit codes.

problems.problem: Migration generates DROP COLUMN instead of RENAME
problems.cause: Prisma doesn't always detect renames. It sees the old column disappear and a new one appear.
problems.solution: Use `--create-only` to generate the migration file without applying it. Edit the SQL to use `ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN` instead of DROP + ADD. Then apply with `npx prisma migrate dev`.

problems.problem: Drift detected: database schema differs from migration history
problems.cause: Someone modified the database directly (outside of migrations), or `prisma db push` was used.
problems.solution: Run `npx prisma migrate dev` which will detect the drift and either reset or create a fix migration. For production, run `npx prisma migrate resolve` to mark the issue as resolved.

problems.problem: Failed migration leaves database in inconsistent state
problems.cause: Migration partially applied before failing. PostgreSQL wraps DDL in transactions, so this is rare, but can happen with data migrations.
problems.solution: Use `npx prisma migrate resolve --rolled-back '20240115_migration_name'` to mark it as rolled back. Fix the migration SQL, then re-apply.

2.5.5.intuition: Prisma Studio and query logging are like a dashboard and black box recorder for your database operations. The studio lets you peek inside the database visually, while query logging records every SQL statement for post-mortem analysis. Together, they take the mystery out of what your ORM is actually doing.

exercises.title: N+1 Detector
exercises.description: Write a middleware or wrapper that detects N+1 query patterns by analyzing the query log. If the same query template runs more than N times in rapid succession, log a warning with the query and suggestion to use include/select.
exercises.hint: Use prisma.$on('query') to track queries. Group by query template (ignoring parameter values). Alert when a template appears > 5 times within 100ms.

exercises.title: Query Performance Dashboard
exercises.description: Build an endpoint that returns query performance metrics: total queries, slow queries (> 50ms), average query time, most frequent queries, and current connection pool status. Reset metrics on demand.
exercises.hint: Collect metrics via prisma.$on('query'). Store in a Map. Expose via a /debug/queries endpoint (protected by an admin check).

exercises.title: Error Handler Integration
exercises.description: Create a comprehensive Prisma error handler that maps all PrismaClientKnownRequestError codes to appropriate HTTP status codes and user-friendly messages. Integrate it as Express middleware.
exercises.hint: Map P2002 to 409 Conflict, P2003 to 400 Bad Request, P2025 to 404 Not Found. Use err.meta for details about which field/constraint failed.

problems.problem: Cannot see which SQL queries Prisma generates
problems.cause: Query logging is not enabled by default.
problems.solution: Pass `log: ['query']` to PrismaClient constructor. For event-based logging with timing, use `{ emit: 'event', level: 'query' }` and `prisma.$on('query', ...)`.

problems.problem: Prisma Studio shows stale data
problems.cause: Prisma Studio caches data and may not reflect very recent changes.
problems.solution: Refresh the page. If using Prisma Studio while your app is also writing to the database, Studio may need a manual refresh to show the latest state.

problems.problem: $queryRaw is vulnerable to SQL injection
problems.cause: Using string concatenation or regular template literals instead of tagged template literals for raw queries.
problems.solution: Always use tagged template literals: `prisma.$queryRaw\`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ${id}\``. The tagged literal parameterizes the values. NEVER use `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` with user input.

2.6.1.intuition: Drizzle is like writing SQL but with TypeScript holding your hand. Your schema IS your types, there is no separate language to learn and no code generation to wait for. It is the closest to raw SQL you can get while still having full type safety, like having a SQL expert checking your queries as you type.

exercises.title: Schema Migration from Prisma
exercises.description: Take a Prisma schema and rewrite it as a Drizzle schema. Include all data types, relations, indexes, and constraints. Compare the two approaches.
exercises.hint: Map Prisma types to Drizzle equivalents: String->text, Int->integer, @id->primaryKey(), @default(uuid())->defaultRandom(), @relation->use the relations() function separately.

exercises.title: Complex Schema with Self-Relations
exercises.description: Design a Drizzle schema for a comments system with nested replies (self-referencing), likes (polymorphic on posts and comments), and user relations. Include proper indexes.
exercises.hint: Self-reference: references(() => comments.id). For polymorphic, use a discriminator column: commentableType + commentableId.

exercises.title: Type-Safe Utility Functions
exercises.description: Create utility functions that leverage Drizzle's type inference: a generic findById<T>, a paginated findMany, and a create function that validates required fields at the type level.
exercises.hint: Use InferSelectModel and InferInsertModel for generic types. Use keyof typeof schema to reference table names dynamically.

problems.problem: Circular reference error in self-referencing tables
problems.cause: TypeScript can't resolve the circular type reference when a table references itself.
problems.solution: Use a type annotation workaround: `references((): any => categories.id)` or use the explicit `AnyPgColumn` type for self-references.

problems.problem: decimal columns return strings instead of numbers
problems.cause: PostgreSQL numeric/decimal types are returned as strings by the pg driver to avoid precision loss.
problems.solution: Parse in your application code: `parseFloat(product.price)`. Or use a custom type mapper in Drizzle's configuration.

problems.problem: Relations not working in queries
problems.cause: Relations defined in a separate file are not passed to the drizzle() constructor.
problems.solution: Import both schema and relations, then spread both into the schema option: `drizzle(pool, { schema: { ...schema, ...relations } })`.

2.6.2.intuition: Drizzle's query API is like writing SQL but with autocomplete and spell-check. Every table name, column name, and operator is checked by TypeScript as you type. It is SQL with training wheels that you never need to remove, because the training wheels make you faster, not slower.

exercises.title: CRUD Repository with Drizzle
exercises.description: Build a complete UserRepository class using Drizzle's core API: create, findById, findByEmail, search (with filters), update, softDelete. Include proper typing for all methods.
exercises.hint: Use InferSelectModel for return types. Use the core API (db.select, db.insert, etc.) for maximum control.

exercises.title: Complex Report Query
exercises.description: Write a Drizzle query that produces a sales report: monthly revenue, order count, unique customers, top product per month. Use CTEs, subqueries, and aggregations.
exercises.hint: Use sql`` template for complex expressions. Chain .from() with subqueries. Use db.select().from(sql`...`) for CTEs.

exercises.title: Core API vs Relational API
exercises.description: Implement the same 5 queries using both the core API (with explicit JOINs) and the relational API (with `with`). Compare the generated SQL, type safety, and readability.
exercises.hint: Enable logging to see generated SQL. The relational API generates separate queries for relations (like Prisma), while the core API uses actual SQL JOINs.

problems.problem: Type error when using operators like eq() with wrong column types
problems.cause: Drizzle enforces strict type checking. Comparing a UUID column with a number, or a decimal with a number literal, causes type errors.
problems.solution: Ensure types match: use strings for uuid and decimal columns. For decimal, pass string values: `eq(products.price, '9.99')` not `eq(products.price, 9.99)`.

problems.problem: Relational queries return empty relations even though data exists
problems.cause: Relations are not defined or not passed to the drizzle() constructor.
problems.solution: Define relations using the `relations()` function, import them, and spread into the schema parameter: `drizzle(pool, { schema: { ...schema, ...relations } })`.

problems.problem: Cannot use .orderBy() with relational queries
problems.cause: The relational API uses a different syntax for ordering than the core API.
problems.solution: In relational queries, use the callback syntax: `orderBy: (table, { desc }) => [desc(table.createdAt)]` instead of the standalone `desc()` function.

2.6.3.intuition: Drizzle Kit is like a translator that watches your TypeScript schema and writes the SQL migration letters for you. You can trust the translations and send them as-is, or review and tweak them before sending. Unlike a black-box translator, you see every word and can change anything.

exercises.title: Full Migration Lifecycle
exercises.description: Create a schema with 4 tables, generate initial migration, apply it, then make 3 schema changes (add column, add table, add index). Generate and apply each migration separately. Test rollback by reversing changes.
exercises.hint: Use `drizzle-kit generate` after each schema change. Review the SQL before applying. Drizzle doesn't auto-generate down migrations, so plan reversals manually.

exercises.title: Introspect and Migrate
exercises.description: Use `drizzle-kit pull` to introspect an existing database. Clean up the generated schema (add proper relation names, fix types). Then make a change and generate a migration.
exercises.hint: Pull generates schema.ts from the database. You'll need to add relations manually. After cleanup, changes will generate proper migration diffs.

exercises.title: CI/CD Migration Script
exercises.description: Write a robust migration script that: checks database connectivity, runs pending migrations with a timeout, logs the results, and exits with appropriate codes. Integrate into a Docker entrypoint.
exercises.hint: Wrap migrate() in a try-catch. Set a connection timeout. Log migration names as they run. Exit with code 1 on failure.

problems.problem: drizzle-kit generate produces no output
problems.cause: The schema file path in drizzle.config.ts doesn't match the actual file location, or there are no changes from the last snapshot.
problems.solution: Verify the schema path in drizzle.config.ts. Check the meta/ folder for the latest snapshot. If starting fresh, delete the drizzle/ folder and regenerate.

problems.problem: Migration fails with 'relation already exists'
problems.cause: The migration was partially applied previously, or the database was modified outside of migrations.
problems.solution: Check the __drizzle_migrations table to see what's been applied. For a fresh start, drop the table and re-run all migrations. For production, manually resolve by editing the migration or marking it as applied.

problems.problem: Drizzle generates destructive migration (DROP COLUMN) for a rename
problems.cause: Drizzle Kit can't detect renames — it sees a column disappear and a new one appear.
problems.solution: Use `--custom` flag or edit the generated SQL to use ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN instead of DROP + ADD. Always review generated SQL before applying.

2.7.1.intuition: Redis data structures are like specialized containers in a workshop. Strings are labeled jars (one thing per jar). Lists are tubes where you push items in one end and pop from the other. Sets are bags of unique marbles. Sorted sets are trophy shelves where each trophy has a score determining its position. Hashes are filing cabinets with labeled drawers. Each container is optimized for specific operations, and choosing the right one makes your work dramatically faster.

exercises.title: Build a Leaderboard
exercises.description: Implement a game leaderboard using Redis sorted sets: addScore(userId, score), getTopN(n), getRank(userId), getAroundMe(userId, range). Handle ties and real-time updates.
exercises.hint: Use ZADD for scores, ZREVRANGE for top N, ZREVRANK for rank, ZREVRANGEBYRANK for surrounding players. Use ZINCRBY for adding to existing scores.

exercises.title: Session Store
exercises.description: Build a Redis-backed session store: createSession(userId) -> sessionToken, getSession(token) -> userData, updateSession(token, data), destroySession(token), cleanExpiredSessions(). Use hashes for session data with 24-hour TTL.
exercises.hint: Use HSET for session data, EXPIRE for TTL, HGETALL to retrieve. Generate random tokens with crypto.randomUUID().

exercises.title: Unique Visitor Counter
exercises.description: Build a system that tracks unique daily visitors per page using sets. Implement: trackVisit(page, userId), getUniqueVisitors(page, date), getMostVisitedPages(date). Automatically expire old data.
exercises.hint: Use SADD visitors:{date}:{page} userId. Use SCARD for counts. Use KEYS or SCAN to find all pages for a date.

problems.problem: Redis returns all values as strings
problems.cause: Redis stores everything as strings internally. Numbers, booleans, and objects are all returned as strings.
problems.solution: Parse values after retrieval: `parseInt(await redis.get('count'))`, `JSON.parse(await redis.get('data'))`. For hashes, all field values are strings too.

problems.problem: KEYS command blocks Redis for seconds
problems.cause: KEYS iterates through ALL keys in the database. With millions of keys, this blocks the single-threaded Redis server.
problems.solution: Never use KEYS in production. Use SCAN with a cursor for safe iteration. Or use naming conventions and direct key access instead of searching.

problems.problem: Memory usage grows unbounded
problems.cause: No TTL set on cached data, or lists/sets growing without limits.
problems.solution: Always set TTL on cache keys. Use LTRIM to cap list sizes. Monitor with `redis-cli INFO memory`. Set maxmemory and eviction policy in redis.conf.

further_reading.title: Redis University
further_reading.note: Free courses on Redis data structures and patterns

2.7.2.intuition: Caching is like keeping a notepad on your desk with answers to frequently asked questions. When someone asks, you check the notepad first (cache hit = instant answer) instead of walking to the filing cabinet every time (database query = slow). The challenge is knowing when the notepad is outdated and needs updating — that is cache invalidation, famously one of the two hard problems in computer science.

exercises.title: Cache Middleware for Express
exercises.description: Build an Express middleware that caches GET responses in Redis. Support configurable TTL per route, cache key based on URL + query params, and a way to bypass cache (X-Skip-Cache header). Include cache hit/miss headers in the response.
exercises.hint: Intercept res.json() to capture the response body. Use the full URL as the cache key. Set X-Cache: HIT or MISS headers.

exercises.title: Stampede-Protected Cache
exercises.description: Implement a caching layer that prevents cache stampedes using the locking pattern. Test by simulating 100 concurrent requests for the same cache key with a 1-second database query. Measure how many DB queries actually execute.
exercises.hint: Use Redis SET NX EX for locks. Waiting requests should poll with exponential backoff. Measure with a counter that tracks DB calls.

exercises.title: Multi-Level Cache
exercises.description: Implement a two-level cache: L1 (in-process Map with 100-item LRU) and L2 (Redis). Reads check L1 first, then L2, then database. Writes update both caches. Handle TTL at both levels.
exercises.hint: Use a Map with size cap for L1. On eviction from L1, the item might still be in L2. Different TTLs: L1 = 60s, L2 = 3600s.

problems.problem: Stale data served after database update
problems.cause: Cache was not invalidated after the database write. The old cached value is still served until TTL expires.
problems.solution: Invalidate (delete) the cache key after every write. Or use write-through caching that updates cache and database together.

problems.problem: Cache stampede crashes the database after a popular key expires
problems.cause: Many concurrent requests all see a cache miss and hit the database simultaneously.
problems.solution: Implement cache locking (only one request rebuilds), stale-while-revalidate, or pre-emptive refresh (refresh cache before TTL expires).

problems.problem: Redis memory usage keeps growing
problems.cause: Keys without TTL accumulate. Or cached values are too large. Or too many unique cache keys are created.
problems.solution: Always set TTL on cache keys. Monitor with `redis-cli INFO memory`. Set maxmemory and maxmemory-policy (allkeys-lru is a good default). Audit key sizes with `redis-cli --bigkeys`.

further_reading.title: Caching Strategies and How to Choose

2.7.3.intuition: Pub/Sub is like a radio broadcast: publishers transmit on a channel and anyone tuned in receives the message. No recording, no replay. Lua scripting is like handing a recipe card to the Redis chef: instead of shouting instructions one at a time from outside the kitchen (which lets others interrupt between steps), you give the chef the complete recipe and they execute all steps without interruption.

exercises.title: Real-Time Notification System
exercises.description: Build a notification system using Redis Pub/Sub: publish events from your API (order.created, user.followed, comment.added), subscribe in a WebSocket server, and forward to connected users. Handle reconnection.
exercises.hint: Use separate Redis connections for pub and sub. In the subscriber, maintain a map of userId -> WebSocket connections. Re-subscribe on disconnect.

exercises.title: Distributed Rate Limiter
exercises.description: Implement a sliding window rate limiter using Lua scripting that works across multiple Node.js instances. Support per-user, per-IP, and per-endpoint limits. Return remaining quota in response headers.
exercises.hint: Use sorted sets with timestamps as scores. The Lua script atomically cleans old entries, counts current, and adds new. Use X-RateLimit-Remaining and X-RateLimit-Reset headers.

exercises.title: Distributed Lock with Fencing
exercises.description: Build a distributed lock using Redis that handles: acquisition with timeout, automatic expiration, safe release (only the owner can release), and lock extension for long operations.
exercises.hint: Use SET NX EX with a unique lock value. Release with Lua that checks the value before deleting. Extend with Lua that checks ownership before updating TTL.

problems.problem: Pub/Sub messages are lost when subscriber disconnects
problems.cause: Redis Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget. Messages are not persisted. If no subscriber is listening, the message is discarded.
problems.solution: For reliable messaging, use Redis Streams instead of Pub/Sub. Streams persist messages and support consumer groups with acknowledgment. Or use a dedicated message queue (BullMQ, RabbitMQ).

problems.problem: Lua script blocks Redis for too long
problems.cause: Lua scripts run atomically, blocking all other Redis operations. A slow script (complex computation, large dataset iteration) freezes Redis.
problems.solution: Keep Lua scripts fast (< 5ms). Avoid iterating over large datasets. Use SCAN in application code instead of Lua for large key spaces. Set lua-time-limit in redis.conf.

problems.problem: Subscriber connection used for regular commands fails
problems.cause: Once a connection enters subscribe mode, it can only use SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, PSUBSCRIBE, PUNSUBSCRIBE, and PING.
problems.solution: Always use a dedicated Redis connection for subscriptions. Create two connections: one for pub/commands, one for subscriptions.

further_reading.title: Distributed Locks with Redis
further_reading.note: The Redlock algorithm for distributed locking

2.7.4.intuition: Pipelining is like sending a shopping list to the store instead of making a separate trip for each item. You hand over the whole list, the store gathers everything, and sends it all back in one delivery. Cluster mode is like having multiple stores in different neighborhoods, each responsible for certain products, so no single store gets overwhelmed.

exercises.title: Bulk Cache Warmer
exercises.description: Write a function that takes an array of 10,000 cache entries and populates Redis using pipelining. Measure the time difference between individual SET commands and pipelined commands. Include error handling for partial failures.
exercises.hint: Pipeline in batches of 1000. Check results array for errors: `results.filter(([err]) => err)`. Use console.time to measure.

exercises.title: Cluster-Aware Cache
exercises.description: Set up a Redis Cluster (using Docker) with 3 master nodes. Build a caching layer that works with the cluster. Handle MOVED and ASK redirects gracefully.
exercises.hint: Use `new Redis.Cluster()`. ioredis handles redirects automatically. Test by getting/setting keys that hash to different nodes.

exercises.title: Redis Health Monitor
exercises.description: Build a health check module that monitors: connection status, memory usage, connected clients, keyspace hits/misses, and latency. Expose as an Express endpoint. Alert when metrics exceed thresholds.
exercises.hint: Use redis.info() to get server stats. Parse the response text. Calculate hit rate from keyspace_hits / (keyspace_hits + keyspace_misses).

problems.problem: Pipeline errors are silently swallowed
problems.cause: Pipeline exec() returns an array where each entry is [error, result]. If you don't check the error, failures are silent.
problems.solution: Always check for errors in pipeline results: `results.forEach(([err, val]) => { if (err) throw err; })`. Or write a helper that throws on any error.

problems.problem: CROSSSLOT error in Redis Cluster
problems.cause: A multi-key operation (MGET, pipeline) accesses keys on different hash slots in a cluster. Redis Cluster requires all keys in a transaction to be on the same node.
problems.solution: Use hash tags to force keys to the same slot: `{user:1}:name` and `{user:1}:email` will be on the same node because the hash is computed from `user:1` only.

problems.problem: Connection pool exhaustion under high load
problems.cause: Not enough connections for the number of concurrent operations. Or connections are leaked by not handling errors.
problems.solution: For single-node: ioredis uses a single connection by default (fine for most cases due to pipelining). For cluster: each node gets its own connection. If you need more throughput, create multiple Redis instances.

2.8.1.intuition: MongoDB is like a filing cabinet where each folder can contain different types of documents. In a relational database, every row must have the exact same columns, like a spreadsheet. In MongoDB, each document can have whatever fields it needs, like a folder that might contain a letter, a photo, or a contract — each with different properties.

exercises.title: Product Catalog CRUD
exercises.description: Build CRUD operations for a product catalog where products have different attributes based on category (electronics have specs.ram, clothing has specs.size). Demonstrate the flexibility of the document model.
exercises.hint: Use nested objects for category-specific attributes. Query with dot notation: {'specs.ram': {$gte: 8}}. Use $exists to find products with specific attributes.

exercises.title: Bulk Operations
exercises.description: Implement a bulk import that: inserts new products, updates existing ones (matched by SKU), and deletes discontinued ones. Use bulkWrite for efficiency. Handle partial failures.
exercises.hint: Use bulkWrite with an array of insertOne, updateOne, and deleteOne operations. Set ordered: false for parallel execution. Check result.insertedCount, modifiedCount, deletedCount.

exercises.title: Query Builder
exercises.description: Build a dynamic query builder for MongoDB that accepts a filter object with: search (text), priceRange, categories (array), inStock (boolean), sortBy, page, limit. Convert to MongoDB query syntax.
exercises.hint: Build the filter object conditionally. Use $text for search (requires text index). Use $in for categories. Combine with $and.

problems.problem: ObjectId comparison fails — documents not found
problems.cause: Comparing a string with an ObjectId. `{ _id: '507f...' }` won't match `{ _id: ObjectId('507f...') }`.
problems.solution: Always convert string IDs to ObjectId: `{ _id: new ObjectId(stringId) }`. Handle invalid ObjectId strings with try-catch.

problems.problem: Query returns all documents instead of filtered results
problems.cause: Empty filter object `{}` matches all documents. A typo in the field name silently matches nothing, causing the $and to be effectively empty.
problems.solution: Log your query filter before executing. Use TypeScript interfaces to catch field name typos. Test with explain() to verify the query plan.

problems.problem: Updates silently do nothing
problems.cause: The filter doesn't match any documents, or the update operators are wrong. updateOne returns modifiedCount: 0 but doesn't throw.
problems.solution: Always check result.matchedCount and modifiedCount. Use findOneAndUpdate with returnDocument: 'after' to see the result. Log the filter and update for debugging.

2.8.2.intuition: The aggregation pipeline is like an assembly line in a factory. Raw materials (documents) enter one end and pass through workstations (stages) that each perform one transformation — sorting, filtering, grouping, reshaping. At the end, you get the finished product (your report or analysis). Each station only cares about what it receives and what it passes on.

exercises.title: E-Commerce Dashboard
exercises.description: Build an aggregation pipeline that produces a complete dashboard: total revenue, order count, average order value, revenue by category, daily revenue for the last 30 days, and top 10 customers by spending. Use $facet for a single query.
exercises.hint: Use $facet with named pipelines. Each pipeline starts with $group or appropriate stage. Use $dateToString for daily grouping.

exercises.title: User Activity Report
exercises.description: Given a 'sessions' collection with userId, startTime, endTime, and pages array, build a pipeline that calculates: average session duration, most visited pages, daily active users, and user engagement tiers (based on session count).
exercises.hint: Use $subtract for duration, $unwind for pages, $group for DAU. Use $bucket for engagement tiers.

exercises.title: Pipeline vs SQL
exercises.description: Take 5 complex SQL queries (with JOINs, GROUP BY, HAVING, subqueries) and rewrite them as MongoDB aggregation pipelines. Compare readability and performance.
exercises.hint: SQL JOIN = $lookup. SQL GROUP BY = $group. SQL HAVING = $match after $group. SQL subquery = $lookup with pipeline.

problems.problem: Aggregation returns empty results
problems.cause: $match stage filters out all documents due to type mismatch (string vs ObjectId), date format issues, or field name typos.
problems.solution: Debug stage by stage: run with only the first stage, check results, add the next stage, repeat. Use `$match: {}` temporarily to bypass filtering.

problems.problem: $lookup returns empty arrays
problems.cause: The localField and foreignField types don't match (e.g., string vs ObjectId), or the field names are wrong.
problems.solution: Verify field types match between collections. If one stores ObjectId and the other stores string, convert with $toObjectId or $toString in the pipeline.

problems.problem: Aggregation runs out of memory on large datasets
problems.cause: The pipeline exceeds the 100MB memory limit per stage (e.g., large $group or $sort).
problems.solution: Pass `{ allowDiskUse: true }` as an option. Also, $match early to reduce data volume, and use $project to reduce document size before heavy stages.

further_reading.title: Practical MongoDB Aggregations
further_reading.note: Free online book with real-world aggregation examples

2.8.3.intuition: Mongoose is like a security guard and personal assistant for your MongoDB data. Without it, anyone can put anything in any format into the database. Mongoose checks every document against the schema rules (the guard), transforms data automatically (lowercase emails, trim whitespace), and runs custom logic on save/delete (the assistant). It trades some of MongoDB's flexibility for safety and convenience.

exercises.title: Complete Blog Model Layer
exercises.description: Build Mongoose models for: User (with auth methods), Post (with slugs, tags, and status), Comment (nested replies), and Like (polymorphic). Include validation, middleware, virtuals, and populate.
exercises.hint: Use pre-save to generate slugs. Use virtual populate for comments on posts. Use discriminators or a commentableType field for polymorphic likes.

exercises.title: Validation Middleware
exercises.description: Create an Express middleware that catches Mongoose ValidationErrors and transforms them into a standardized API error response with field-level error messages. Handle duplicate key errors (11000) too.
exercises.hint: Check for mongoose.Error.ValidationError and error code 11000. Map each validation error to { field, message }. Return 422 for validation, 409 for duplicates.

exercises.title: Soft Delete Plugin
exercises.description: Build a Mongoose plugin that adds soft delete functionality to any model: adds deletedAt field, overrides find/findOne to exclude deleted, adds restore() method, adds forceDelete() method.
exercises.hint: A plugin receives (schema, options). Use schema.add for new fields. Use pre('find') to add { deletedAt: null }. Add instance methods for restore/forceDelete.

problems.problem: Populate returns null even though the referenced document exists
problems.cause: The ref value in the schema doesn't match the model name, or the stored ID is a string instead of an ObjectId.
problems.solution: Verify that `ref: 'ModelName'` matches exactly what you passed to `model('ModelName', schema)`. Check that IDs are stored as ObjectId, not strings.

problems.problem: Validation doesn't run on findByIdAndUpdate
problems.cause: By default, Mongoose doesn't run validators on update operations. Only save() triggers validators.
problems.solution: Pass `{ runValidators: true }` as an option: `findByIdAndUpdate(id, update, { runValidators: true })`.

problems.problem: Pre-save middleware runs but this.isModified() is always true
problems.cause: Using create() or save() on a new document — all fields are considered 'modified' on the first save.
problems.solution: Check `this.isNew` to distinguish between create and update. For password hashing, combine: `if (this.isModified('password') && !this.isNew) { ... }` — or simply always hash if modified.
